Digital Humanities · Victorian Criminal Justice · Old Bailey Proceedings

Women at the Old Bailey, 1837–1901

A data-driven analysis of gender, prosecution, and punishment

83,994 Trial accounts
106,152 Defendants
781 Court sessions
About Key Findings Ten Phases Phase 10 Analysis
The Project

What This Is

A trial-level dataset was built from scratch from the full Victorian Old Bailey XML corpus, covering every court session from 1837 to 1901. Ten phases of analysis were conducted, moving from baseline gender patterns through offence composition, punishment severity, and robustness testing, and ending in close institutional analysis of specific offences.

The goal throughout was to test whether observed gender disparities held up under scrutiny — and if so, what was actually producing them. The answer, consistently, was that aggregate statistics don't interpret themselves: the patterns arose from specific legal categories, the evidentiary conditions those categories created, and the institutional frameworks through which individual prosecutions were decided.

This is independent research conducted through digital humanities rather than through a history department. The dataset, the analytical decisions, and the interpretations are original. The work is grounded throughout in the primary record — individual trial transcripts were read closely wherever the structured data raised questions the metadata alone couldn't answer.

Results

Key Findings

Female share, 1837–1901
22% → 9.7%
A 60% relative decline
Women represented nearly one in four defendants in the late 1830s. By 1901, fewer than one in ten. Total caseloads fell ~35% — the female share fell twice as fast. A genuine compositional shift, not a caseload artefact.
Structural finding
Not uniform
Disparities are offence-specific
Gender gaps in conviction and sentencing disappear or reverse when offence composition is controlled. The differences arise from specific offence–punishment configurations, not from a single institutional logic applied to all women.
Path A — Robbery 1880–81
Negative result
The anomaly dissolves
Female over-representation in a male-typed imprisonment regime dissolves under four structural tests. Evidentiary strength — identification reliability, clear account of who took what — predicted outcomes regardless of sex.
Path B — Bigamy, 1,332 defendants
d ≈ 0.85
Men convicted more, sentenced harder
Male conviction rate 89% vs 74% female. Three-level punishment asymmetry: women excluded from transportation and penal servitude entirely. Female median sentence 1 month; male median 6 months. The gap was large, persistent, and reflects the legal architecture of Victorian marriage — not the moral policing of women.
Methodology

Ten Phases of Analysis

1–2Dataset construction from raw XML corpus and trial-level enrichment — attaching offence, verdict, and punishment to every defendant with 100% match rate
3Baseline gender patterns: offence composition, conviction rates, and punishment outcomes across 106,152 defendants
4Robustness testing across three historical periods (Early, Mid, Late Victorian) and two severity definitions — confirming that findings survive temporal and definitional variation
5–6Female criminality in depth: offence composition, punishment configurations, and rarity profiling across 17,564 female defendants
7–8Structural divergence mapping: identifying which offence–punishment combinations showed persistent gender asymmetry across the full period
9Outlier targeting — statistical identification of the cases most likely to yield substantive historical insight, using surprise scoring and diversified extraction
10Institutional testing: two close analyses — violent robbery 1880–81 (Path A) and bigamy across the full Victorian period (Path B)
Phase 10 — Detailed Analysis

Institutional Testing: The Visual Summaries

Phase 10 · A

Path A — Violent Theft Anomaly

How an apparent gender anomaly in robbery prosecutions dissolves under four structural tests

Phase 10 · B

Path B — Rape: Accessory Liability

Why women showed higher conviction rates in rape trials — and why that reflects legal role, not harsher treatment

Phase 10 · B

Path B — Bigamy: Conviction Patterns

How bigamy ran against the 'harsher on women' thesis: men convicted more often, sentenced more severely

Phase 10 · B — Detail

Bigamy — Custodial Severity Structure

The three-level gender inequality in punishment, its persistence across all three Victorian periods, and the escalation mechanism
